problem.title: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
problem.description: Given the root of a binary tree, flatten the tree into a "linked list" in-place. The "linked list" should use the same TreeNode class where the right child pointer points to the next node in the list and the left child pointer is always null.
assumptions: Flatten in-place — don't create new nodes.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Recursive (Reverse Preorder) [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: This approach processes the tree in reverse preorder (right, left, root) and threads nodes together using a trailing pointer. By visiting right before left, each node's right pointer can be safely rewired to the previously visited node, effectively building the flattened list from back to front. The key insight is that reverse preorder naturally yields the correct linking order without needing extra storage.
solutions.code_python:
# class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0): self.val=val; self.left=self.right=None
from typing import Optional

def flatten(root: Optional['TreeNode']) -> None:
    def helper(node: Optional['TreeNode'], prev: Optional['TreeNode']) -> Optional['TreeNode']:
        if not node:
            return prev
        prev = helper(node.right, prev)
        prev = helper(node.left, prev)
        node.right = prev
        node.left = None
        return node

    helper(root, None)

complexity.space: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Iterative with Stack [✓ Interviewer Accepted]
solutions.description: This approach uses an explicit stack to simulate preorder traversal and rewires right pointers on the fly. Pushing right before left ensures left children are processed first (LIFO), and each popped node's right pointer is set to the next node on the stack. The key insight is that a stack cleanly mirrors the recursive call structure of preorder traversal.
